audit_status.source_base: Hebrew 1 Samuel 6 draft aligned to guilt offering, cow/cart test, ark return, Beth-shemesh offering and deaths, and Kiriath-jearim request; maximum textual audit required.
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for Hebrew-first naming, restored/mystical separation, narrative ambiguity, institutional power, vulnerable persons, violence, and misuse risks.

verses.restored: Ark of YHWH was in field of Philistines seven months.
Philistines called for priests and diviners, saying, What shall we do with ark of YHWH? Make known to us with what we shall send it to its place.

They said, If you send away ark of God of Israel, do not send it empty, but returning, return to him a guilt offering. Then you may be healed, and it will be known to you why his hand does not turn aside from you.

They said, What guilt offering shall we return to him? They said, According to number of lords of Philistines: five golden tumors and five golden mice, for one plague is upon all of you and upon your lords.
You shall make images of your tumors and images of your mice that ruin land, and give glory to God of Israel. Perhaps he will lighten his hand from upon you, your gods, and your land.
Why should you make your hearts heavy as Egyptians and Pharaoh made their hearts heavy? Was it not after he dealt severely with them that they sent them away and they went?
Now take and make one new cart, and two milk cows upon whom no yoke has gone up. Bind cows to cart, and return their calves home from behind them.
Take ark of YHWH and put it upon cart. Put vessels of gold that you return to him as guilt offering in box at its side, send it, and let it go.
Watch: if it goes up by way of its territory to Beth-shemesh, he has done to us this great evil. But if not, then we will know that it was not his hand that struck us; by chance it happened to us.
Humans did so. They took two milk cows, bound them to cart, and shut their calves in house.
They set ark of YHWH upon cart, and box with golden mice and images of their tumors.
Cows went straight on way toward Beth-shemesh. On one highway they went, lowing as they went, and did not turn right or left. Lords of Philistines walked after them as far as border of Beth-shemesh.
Beth-shemesh was reaping wheat harvest in valley. They lifted their eyes, saw ark, and rejoiced to see it.
Cart came into field of Joshua the Beth-shemite and stood there. A great stone was there. They split wood of cart and offered cows as an ascending offering to YHWH.

Levites took down ark of YHWH and box that was with it, in which were vessels of gold, and set them upon great stone. Humans of Beth-shemesh offered ascending offerings and sacrificed sacrifices on that day to YHWH.
Five lords of Philistines saw and returned to Ekron on that day.
These are golden tumors that Philistines returned as guilt offering to YHWH: for Ashdod one, for Gaza one, for Ashkelon one, for Gath one, for Ekron one.

And golden mice were according to number of all cities of Philistines belonging to five lords, from fortified city to village of open country. Great stone upon which they rested ark of YHWH is witness to this day in field of Joshua the Beth-shemite.
He struck among humans of Beth-shemesh because they looked into/at ark of YHWH; he struck among people seventy humans. The people mourned because YHWH had struck people with a great striking.
Humans of Beth-shemesh said, Who is able to stand before YHWH, this holy God? And to whom shall he go up from upon us?
They sent messengers to inhabitants of Kiriath-jearim, saying, Philistines have returned ark of YHWH. Come down and bring it up to yourselves.

translation_notes: Philistine priests and diviners are portrayed as genuine advisers within their community. The text need not be read as permission to ridicule non-Israelite religious expertise.
Golden tumors and mice are mimetic guilt offerings. They represent acknowledged harm but are not magical medical treatment or a substitute for public health.
The Egypt comparison belongs to Israelite narrative memory and should not fuel anti-Egyptian racism or simplistic equations between ancient and modern peoples.
The test uses nursing cows separated from calves, then kills the cows as sacrifice. Animal distress is audible in the narrative and must not be romanticized or repeated.
Omens and chance language reflect ancient discernment. They do not provide a safe method for making high-stakes modern decisions.

Beth-shemesh harvesters rejoice, but handling of the ark leads to a difficult death report. Textual witnesses differ dramatically: the Masoretic wording is commonly read as seventy, while other traditions preserve much larger or differently structured numbers.
Verse 19's reason may be looking at or into the ark, and textual details require maximum audit. The deaths cannot be made ethically simple by invoking holiness.
Collective striking of a community must not justify collective punishment, sacred terror, or claims that accidental ritual mistakes deserve death.
The Beth-shemesh question arises from trauma and fear. Holiness should not be taught as Divine hostility toward ordinary human bodies.
Sending the ark to Kiriath-jearim again transfers responsibility; consent and capacity of receiving communities matter.
Gold offered after harm does not restore dead people, heal trauma automatically, or purchase immunity.
Philistine and Israelite communities both respond to uncertainty through religious interpretation; neither should be caricatured as primitive.
safety_note: Do not imitate animal-distressing omen tests, use gold as magical medicine, or justify collective death for ritual error. Do not ridicule Philistine religion or treat uncertain plague language as medical fact. The Beth-shemesh deaths and textual variants require humility, trauma awareness, and rejection of sacred-terror preaching.
